questions.text: Which of the following is a fiat-collateralized stablecoin?

options.text: DAI - backed by over-collateralized ETH in MakerDAO Vaults.
USDC - each token is backed 1:1 by US dollars held in regulated bank accounts.
UST (TerraUSD) - relied on an algorithmic mint-burn mechanism with LUNA.
MSTC - the native volatile coin of MST Blockchain, not a stablecoin.

questions.explanation: USDC is the classic fiat-collateralized stablecoin. DAI is crypto-collateralized. UST was algorithmic. MSTC is not a stablecoin at all.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: B

USDC is the classic fiat-collateralized stablecoin. DAI is crypto-collateralized. UST was algorithmic. MSTC is not a stablecoin at all.

questions.text: What is the minimum collateralization ratio for an ETH-backed DAI Vault in MakerDAO?

options.text: 100% - you can mint exactly as much DAI as the ETH value you deposit.
150% - you must deposit at least $150 worth of ETH to mint $100 DAI.
200% - a common misconception, but it's 150% for ETH.
50% - this would be under-collateralized and immediately liquidated.

questions.explanation: The MakerDAO protocol requires a minimum 150% collateralization ratio for ETH Vaults. This over-collateralization protects against ETH price volatility and ensures the system remains solvent.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: B

The MakerDAO protocol requires a minimum 150% collateralization ratio for ETH Vaults. This over-collateralization protects against ETH price volatility and ensures the system remains solvent.

questions.text: How does the Reserve Bank of India's Digital Rupee (e₹) fundamentally differ from a private stablecoin like USDC?

options.text: The Digital Rupee runs on a public blockchain, while USDC runs on a private one.
The Digital Rupee is legal tender - a direct liability of the RBI; USDC is a private IOU.
The Digital Rupee can be traded on DEXs; USDC cannot.
There is no fundamental difference - both are digital representations of fiat currency.

questions.explanation: A CBDC is legal tender, meaning it must be accepted for the settlement of debts and is backed by the sovereign. USDC is a private company's IOU. The legal status and backing are fundamentally different, regardless of the underlying blockchain.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: B

A CBDC is legal tender, meaning it must be accepted for the settlement of debts and is backed by the sovereign. USDC is a private company's IOU. The legal status and backing are fundamentally different, regardless of the underlying blockchain.

questions.text: Why did TerraUSD (UST) collapse in May 2022?

options.text: The US government banned it and seized all reserves.
It was an algorithmic stablecoin with no collateral; a large sell-off triggered a death spiral, hyperinflating LUNA and destroying the peg.
The Ethereum network experienced a hard fork that made UST incompatible.
Circle (issuer of USDC) attacked UST to eliminate competition.

questions.explanation: UST had no real collateral. It relied on a mint-burn algorithm with LUNA. When confidence broke and large UST sell-offs occurred, the algorithm minted massive LUNA to absorb the pressure, crashing LUNA and destroying the peg in an irreversible death spiral.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: B

UST had no real collateral. It relied on a mint-burn algorithm with LUNA. When confidence broke and large UST sell-offs occurred, the algorithm minted massive LUNA to absorb the pressure, crashing LUNA and destroying the peg in an irreversible death spiral.

questions.text: Which feature of MST Blockchain makes it particularly suitable for hosting a retail CBDC like the Digital Rupee?

options.text: Its native coin MSTC is pegged to the Indian Rupee.
Its hybrid architecture allows a permissioned zone for regulated bank nodes and a public zone for open innovation, with ultra-low gas fees across both.
It requires no validators, so transactions are free for CBDC users.
It is the only blockchain approved by the RBI for CBDC pilots.

questions.explanation: MST's hybrid L1 with separate permissioned and public zones is uniquely suited for CBDCs. The RBI can deploy the e₹ token in a permissioned zone with authorized bank validators, while the public zone hosts DeFi and private stablecoins - all sharing the same low-fee infrastructure.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: B

MST's hybrid L1 with separate permissioned and public zones is uniquely suited for CBDCs. The RBI can deploy the e₹ token in a permissioned zone with authorized bank validators, while the public zone hosts DeFi and private stablecoins - all sharing the same low-fee infrastructure.

questions.text: "A crypto-collateralized stablecoin like DAI is inherently safer than a fiat-collateralized stablecoin like USDC because DAI is fully decentralized and therefore cannot fail."
questions.explanation: 1 mark: Correctly states FALSE.+1 mark: Justification explains at least two failure modes for DAI (e.g., ETH crash outpacing liquidations, oracle failure, congestion preventing Keepers) and acknowledges that USDC has different risks (centralization, bank failure). Must not claim DAI is risk-free. Referencing a real event like Black Thursday earns full marks.
questions.modelAnswer:
📊 Marks Breakdown

1 mark: Correctly states FALSE.
+1 mark: Justification explains at least two failure modes for DAI (e.g., ETH crash outpacing liquidations, oracle failure, congestion preventing Keepers) and acknowledges that USDC has different risks (centralization, bank failure). Must not claim DAI is risk-free. Referencing a real event like Black Thursday earns full marks.

questions.text: "India's Digital Rupee (e₹) can be freely traded on decentralized exchanges (DEXs) like any other ERC-20 token, because it is built on blockchain technology."
questions.explanation: 1 mark: Correctly states FALSE.+1 mark: Justification explains that the Digital Rupee runs on a permissioned blockchain with RBI control, that DEXs are permissionless public platforms, and that regulatory restrictions prevent e₹ from being freely traded on DEXs. Must not assume future bridging possibilities or treat "blockchain" as automatically permissionless.
questions.modelAnswer:
📊 Marks Breakdown

1 mark: Correctly states FALSE.
+1 mark: Justification explains that the Digital Rupee runs on a permissioned blockchain with RBI control, that DEXs are permissionless public platforms, and that regulatory restrictions prevent e₹ from being freely traded on DEXs. Must not assume future bridging possibilities or treat "blockchain" as automatically permissionless.

questions.text: Analyze a MakerDAO Vault and determine whether it will be liquidated, and calculate the Keeper's profit if it is.
